header.p: A complete, step-by-step guide to every screen, every button and every situation you'll encounter as a SUDUD Hosting customer. Nothing assumed — if it's a click you'll make, it's in here.

div.span: How to use this manual. The table of contents on the left is sticky — it follows you as you scroll. Click any entry to jump straight to that step. Every section is self-contained, so you can read only the part you need.

section.p: SUDUD Hosting gives you a private Odoo instance on your own subdomain (e.g. my-company.sudud.com) with full admin access. Think of it as renting a ready-to-use Odoo environment that we keep running 24/7 on cloud infrastructure — you get the keys, we handle the hosting.
What's included with every hosting instance:

ul.li: A private subdomain like your-name.sudud.com, secured with a Let's Encrypt SSL certificate (the green padlock in the browser).
One or more Odoo databases you create on demand. You decide how many (within your plan's storage limits) and what they're called.
Full admin rights inside every database — install apps, configure users, customize fields, the works.
Your custom modules can be deployed via a GitHub repository link — we pull and install them for you.
Optional daily snapshots of everything on the instance, plus the ability to take ad-hoc backups of any single database on demand.

section.p: What we manage so you don't have to:

ul.li: Server provisioning and patching.
The web server / proxy and SSL certificate renewal.
The Odoo Docker image and Python dependencies.
The PostgreSQL database server.
Backup storage in encrypted cloud storage.

img.alt: SUDUD home page hero with the Get Started call-to-action

figure.figcaption: The home page is where every customer journey starts.

section.p: You need a free account before you can buy anything or start a trial. Sign-up takes about a minute.

ol.li: From any public page, click Sign Up in the top right of the navigation bar.

On the sign-up form, enter:
Full name — used on your invoices, so put your real name or company name.
Work email — where every notification (sign-in links, invoices, snapshot status, password resets) is delivered. Use a real address you'll actually read.
Password — at least 8 characters. The strength meter below the field turns from red to green as you type; aim for at least "Strong".
Click Create account. We send a verification email to the address you entered.
Open that email and click the Confirm my email button. Your account is active immediately after the click.

img.alt: The Sign Up form filled in halfway with the password strength meter visible

figure.figcaption: Sign-up form. The strength meter updates live; you can't submit until it reaches at least "Fair".

div.span: No verification email? Wait a minute, then check your spam folder. If still nothing, sign in anyway — the "Resend verification" link lets you re-trigger the email.

ol.li: Click Sign In in the top right of any public page.
Enter the email and password you set during sign-up.
Click Sign In. You'll land on your personal dashboard — a list of your instances if you have any, otherwise the home page.

section.p: Once signed in, your avatar (initials) appears in the top-right corner. Click it to open the user menu, where you can reach My Instances, your profile, and the sign-out link.

ol.li: On the Sign In page click Forgot password? below the password field.
Enter the email address linked to your account and click Send reset link.
Check that inbox for an email titled "Reset your SUDUD password". Click the link inside (valid for 1 hour).
Type a new password twice and submit. You're signed in automatically.

div.span: This resets your account password — the one you use to log into the SUDUD customer portal. It is not the same as the admin password inside each of your Odoo databases. For that, see section 4.7.

section.p: If you'd like to test the platform before committing, we offer a time-limited free trial — usually 14 days — with no card required. The trial gives you a real hosting instance on a modest default plan, with the same admin access a paying customer gets.
Eligibility is one trial per customer. When you sign up, if you're eligible the home page shows a green banner across the top inviting you to start it.

img.alt: Green trial banner at the top of the home page

figure.figcaption: The trial banner only appears for eligible accounts.

section.p: Click the banner's Start free trial button to jump into the plan builder with trial mode pre-selected. The rest of the flow is identical to a paid order, except there's no payment screen at the end — your instance starts provisioning the moment you submit.

div.span: Trial limitations. Trial instances don't include daily snapshots (paid add-on) and may have a slightly reduced resource ceiling. Upgrade to a paid plan before your trial ends if you want your data to keep running — once the trial expires, the instance is suspended and eventually wiped. We email you several reminders as the expiry approaches.

section.p: From the home page or top navigation, click Hosting. You'll see a hero section with the price ladder, then the plan builder where the real configuration happens. Below it: optional sections for daily snapshots, custom Python packages, and a GitHub repository.
Your subdomain becomes the URL where your Odoo lives: <subdomain>.sudud.com. You'll also use it to identify your instance in the customer portal.

ul.li: Lowercase letters, digits and single hyphens only. Examples: my-company, acme, shop-v2.
Must start with a letter and end with a letter or digit.
No consecutive hyphens (my--company is rejected).
Length: 3–63 characters.
Reserved names (e.g. www, admin, api) aren't accepted.

section.p: As you type, we check availability live and show a green check ✓ if it's free or a red X if it's already taken or invalid. Pick something you can remember and that represents your business well — you can't rename a subdomain later without cancelling and starting over.

img.alt: Subdomain field showing a live availability check with a green check

figure.figcaption: Live availability check — green when free, red when taken or invalid.

section.p: The plan builder converts your resource choices into a monthly or yearly price. It has three knobs:
A "worker" is one independent process that handles incoming web requests. More workers means more concurrent users served smoothly.

ul.li: Each extra worker also adds CPU and RAM to your instance — the relationship is fixed by the platform (typically ~0.5 vCPU and 512 MB RAM per worker).
For 1–5 active users at once, 2 workers is usually plenty. For 10–25 active users, 4 workers. Heavy usage (reports, imports, scheduled jobs) benefits from more.
You can always change this later — see section 6.2.

section.p: How much disk space your databases, attachments and custom modules can consume. Measured in gigabytes.

ul.li: 5 GB is a comfortable starter for a small business without heavy attachments.
Lots of PDFs, photos or scanned documents? Budget 20+ GB.
Going over this limit doesn't break your instance immediately — we charge a per-GB overage rate and notify you so you can clean up or upgrade.

section.p: Choose Monthly or Yearly. Yearly billing gives you roughly 20% off the equivalent monthly price — the exact discount is shown on the comparison toggle. You're invoiced once per cycle.
The order-summary panel on the right updates live as you adjust the sliders: you see the line items (workers, storage, optional add-ons), the subtotal, and the total due today.

img.alt: Plan builder with workers and storage sliders, billing-period toggle, and live order summary

figure.figcaption: Plan builder. The order summary on the right is your single source of truth for what you'll be charged.

section.p: Below the plan builder, you'll find an optional Add daily snapshots checkbox.

A snapshot is a complete, point-in-time copy of everything on your instance — every database, every uploaded file or attachment, your custom modules and your instance settings — packaged into a single restorable unit. We take one every night at 03:00 UTC, store it encrypted in the cloud, and keep up to the last 7. The oldest is dropped automatically when an 8th arrives.
Why opt in:

ul.li: One click rolls your whole instance back to any of the last 7 days.
Restoration is in-place — no need to re-deploy or reconfigure anything.
Encrypted with a per-instance key you don't have to manage.

section.p: Daily snapshots are billed monthly as a separate add-on, even if your main plan is yearly. You can also enable them after purchase — see section 5.4.

div.span: Strongly recommended for production use. The cost is small relative to what you'd lose if something went wrong without snapshots in place.

section.p: Some Odoo modules — especially custom or third-party ones — require extra Python packages from PyPI (e.g. phonenumbers, openpyxl). You can list them here, one per line, and we'll install them into your Odoo environment before your instance starts.
Skip this section entirely if you don't know whether you need it — you can add packages later from your instance page and it's harmless to leave empty.

ul.li: One package name per line.
Optional version pin with ==, e.g. openpyxl==3.1.2.
Blank lines and # comments are ignored.

section.p: We install with pip on every provision and update. If a package fails to install (typo, missing wheel, etc.) we log the error and continue without it — your instance still comes up.
If you have your own Odoo modules in GitHub, you can have us clone the repo and place the modules in your instance's addons path.

ol.li: Enter the HTTPS repository URL (e.g. https://github.com/your-org/your-modules).
Specify the branch — defaults to main.
If the repository is private, paste a personal access token with read-only access to that one repo. We store it encrypted and only use it to clone and pull.

section.p: After deployment, the modules become available in the Odoo Apps menu. To update them later, you have two options:

ul.li: Auto-update via webhook — we register a webhook on your repository so that every push to the chosen branch triggers a pull on your instance.
Manual pull — click Pull latest from the Repositories section on your instance page whenever you want.

div.span: Updating modules doesn't run database migrations automatically. If a pulled update needs an Odoo module upgrade, use the in-app Apps → Update Module flow, or Repair Feature as a fallback.

ol.li: Click Continue to payment at the bottom of the plan builder.
Review the order summary one more time. If anything's wrong, click Back and adjust.
Enter your card details in the secure payment widget. Card data goes directly to the payment provider — we never see or store your card number.
Click Pay now. The page may briefly redirect to your bank for 3-D Secure verification — this is normal.

img.alt: Checkout page with order summary on the left and payment widget on the right

section.p: As soon as your payment confirms, the portal redirects you to a provisioning page. Five things happen, automatically, in this order:

ol.li: We allocate a server slot and a database server slot for your instance.
We obtain a Let's Encrypt SSL certificate for your subdomain.
We deploy your Odoo container with the resources you bought, plus any custom Python packages or repositories you configured.
We initialize an empty Odoo environment ready for you to create your first database.
We configure the web proxy so your-subdomain.sudud.com points to your container.

section.p: The provisioning page polls every few seconds and refreshes itself automatically when your instance is ready (typically 2–5 minutes for a fresh instance, longer if you supplied a lot of custom packages or a large repo). Once the status flips to Running, you land on your instance dashboard.

img.alt: Provisioning page with the five-step progress indicator

section.p: The instance detail page is your control centre for one specific hosting instance. Reach it from My Instances (in the avatar dropdown, top right) by clicking the instance card.
It's laid out in two columns:

ul.li: Left (main) column: instance info card, in-flight banners (provisioning / payment / restore / trial expiring / snapshot prompt), quick-action buttons, repositories section, custom Python packages section, recent activity.
Right (side) column: recent invoices.

img.alt: Instance detail page with status pill, action buttons, repos section, and invoices column

figure.figcaption: Instance detail page. Sections appear or disappear based on your instance's state and add-ons.

section.p: The coloured pill next to your subdomain tells you exactly what state your instance is in.

tr.td: Your instance is up and serving traffic.
Open it, manage databases, take backups, change plan, stop it.
We're setting up infrastructure (deploy, restore or repair).
Wait — the page auto-refreshes when it's done.
You stopped the instance manually. No traffic served.
Start it back up. Databases stay intact.
Auto-suspended due to an overdue payment or expired trial.
Pay the outstanding invoice (or upgrade from trial) to reactivate.
Order placed, payment not yet confirmed.
Wait for payment confirmation, or pay the invoice manually.
Cancelled. Most data deleted; the most recent snapshot is retained.
Reactivate (and optionally restore the retained snapshot) — see section 8.3.

section.p: Below the info card, you'll see a row of large action buttons. Which ones appear depends on the instance state.

ul.li: Open Instance — opens your Odoo (your-subdomain.sudud.com) in a new tab. Available when Running.
Manage Databases — takes you to the per-database management page. Available when Running.
View Snapshots — opens the snapshots / daily-backups page. Always available for hosting instances (it doubles as the entry point to enable daily snapshots).
View Activity — live tail of your instance's activity log. Available when Running.
Stop / Start — pauses or resumes your instance. Available in the matching states.
Change Plan — adjust workers / storage. Available for paid instances.
Upgrade Plan — trial instances only; converts the trial to a paid subscription.
Reactivate Instance — cancelled instances only; brings the instance back with a new subscription.

section.p: When something is happening in the background (provisioning, a payment clearing, a restore running, a trial expiring soon, daily snapshots offered), you'll see a coloured banner near the top of the page:

ul.li: Blue — informational; usually with a spinner and a "page refreshes automatically" hint.
Yellow — action required (pay an invoice, complete payment for the snapshot add-on, etc.).
Green — payment received, restore complete, etc.
Red — failure that needs attention; the text spells out what's wrong.

section.h3: 4.1 Instance vs database — what's the difference?
section.p: Think of your hosting instance as a building, and your databases as the apartments inside it. The building has plumbing, electricity and a front door (your-subdomain.sudud.com); each apartment is a self-contained Odoo environment with its own data, users and installed apps.
You can have multiple databases on one instance to, for example:

ul.li: Separate two companies that share an admin team.
Keep a sandbox database alongside production so you can test risky changes safely.
Run a staging environment for a development team.

section.p: Each database has its own admin user, its own installed apps, and its own attachments. They share the underlying instance's resources (workers, storage, CPU, RAM).

ol.li: From the instance detail page, click Manage Databases.
You'll see a table of every database currently on this instance. Each row shows the database name (e.g. acme_production) plus a row of action buttons on the right.

img.alt: Databases page with several rows and the per-row action buttons visible

figure.figcaption: Databases page. Each row has six possible buttons: Open, Backup Now, Repair Feature, Reset Password, Duplicate, Delete.

section.p: Database names are prefixed automatically with your subdomain to avoid clashes across customers (so on the wire a database created as prod on instance acme becomes acme_prod).
Rules for the part you type:

ul.li: Lowercase letters, digits, single hyphens or underscores between groups.
Starts with a letter, ends with a letter or digit.
No consecutive hyphens or underscores.
3 to 40 characters.

ol.li: At the top of the Databases page, click Create Database.

Fill in the modal:
Database name — the part after your subdomain prefix. The prefix is shown in grey to the left of the field so you can see the final name.
Admin login — defaults to your account email. This is the user you'll sign in as inside that specific database.
Admin password — at least 6 characters. Keep it somewhere safe — if you forget it, you can reset it from the portal (section 4.7).
Language — the default interface language. English, Arabic, French, Spanish, German available out of the box. Other languages can be added from inside Odoo later.
Click Create. A full-screen overlay appears ("Creating database…") and the page refreshes itself when the database is ready — usually about a minute.

img.alt: Create database modal with all four fields filled in

div.span: Why is creation slow the first time? The very first database on a fresh instance triggers a one-time setup that takes around a minute. Every subsequent database is a near-instant clone of that template, so creation drops to a few seconds.

ol.li: Click Open on the database row. A new tab opens at your-subdomain.sudud.com/web?db=<name>.
Enter the admin login and password you set when creating the database.
You're in! From here on, everything happens inside Odoo — adding companies, installing apps, creating users, importing data, etc.

section.p: Useful when you want to make a fresh copy of an existing database — for example, to test a risky import on a sandbox before running it in production.

ol.li: Click Duplicate on the source database row.
Type a new name for the copy. By default we suggest <source>-copy; change it to whatever you want (same naming rules as section 4.3).
Click Duplicate. The button spins until the copy is ready, then the page refreshes automatically. The original is untouched.

img.alt: Duplicate database modal with the suggested -copy suffix in the target field

section.p: Forgot your admin password for one of your databases? Reset it yourself in seconds — no support ticket, no current password needed.

ol.li: Click Reset Password on the database row.
The modal opens with a blue information banner that says Admin login for this database: your.email@example.com. If you forgot the username too, this tells you what it is.
Enter a new password twice (at least 6 characters).
Click Reset password. The button spins briefly, then the page reloads with a green confirmation banner naming the login and the database that was reset.
Sign in to the database with the login from the banner and the new password.

img.alt: Reset Password modal showing the blue admin-login banner and two password fields

div.span: Reset Password only touches the original admin user of the database — other users you've created inside Odoo are not affected. If you need to reset a different user's password, log in as admin and do it from Settings → Users inside Odoo.

section.p: Sometimes a recent app install, module update or data migration leaves a database in a broken state where every page returns an error and you can't even reach the in-app Apps menu to fix it. Repair Feature is your recovery hatch.

ol.li: Click Repair Feature on the affected database row.

The modal explains what happens. Enter the name of the feature to re-install in the text field. Common picks:
base — rebuilds the Odoo core. Safe, fast, usually enough to recover from minor errors.
web — rebuilds the user-interface shell. Use this if Odoo loads but pages render incorrectly.
all — re-runs every installed feature's update. Slowest but the broadest fix.
A specific feature like account, sale, stock if you know which one started failing.

Click Run upgrade. A full-screen overlay appears ("Repairing your database…"). Your instance is briefly paused, the feature is re-installed, then the instance comes back up. The page reloads automatically.
After the reload, a green banner appears with a collapsible Show full report. Open it to see the technical detail of what just happened — useful if anything's still off.

img.alt: Repair Feature modal with the feature name input field and the list of common picks

div.span: Your instance is unavailable for 30 seconds to a few minutes while the repair runs — every database on the instance is offline during that window, not just the one you're repairing. Don't close the tab; the page will refresh itself.

section.p: Permanent and irreversible. Make sure you have a fresh Backup Now if there's anything in the database you might want back.

ol.li: Click Delete on the database row.

To confirm, retype the full database name (including the subdomain prefix) into the confirmation field. This guard is here on purpose — to make accidentally deleting the wrong database almost impossible.
Click Delete permanently. The button spins until the database and every on-demand backup for it are gone, then the page refreshes.

img.alt: Delete database confirmation modal with the retype-name field

section.p: SUDUD gives you two completely different backup features. Knowing when to use which saves you a lot of time.

tr.td: Every database + uploaded files + custom modules + instance settings.
You click "Backup Now" whenever you want.
Yes — you get a zip file you can store anywhere.
Disaster recovery; "I deleted the wrong thing yesterday".

ol.li: On the Databases page, click Backup Now on the database row you want to back up.

The button spins, and a blue Backing up badge appears next to the database name. The backup runs in the background; the page polls every few seconds and refreshes when it's done. Typical times: 5 seconds for a tiny database, up to a few minutes for a multi-GB one.
Once ready, the badge turns orange and reads Backup ready. A Download Backup button appears in the actions column.

section.p: Click Download Backup. The link is a pre-signed direct download from our cloud storage — it goes straight to your computer, not through the SUDUD server. The file is a standard Odoo backup zip you can re-upload anywhere that runs Odoo.
Backups roll off automatically after 24 hours, but you can free the slot earlier by clicking Discard. This deletes the cloud copy immediately. Discarded backups can't be recovered.

Only one on-demand backup per instance can be "ready" at a time. While one is ready (orange badge), Backup Now buttons on every database row are disabled. To take a fresh one, either Discard the existing one first, or click Backup Now anyway — you'll be asked to confirm "this will replace the existing backup for X. Continue?".

img.alt: A database row showing the in-progress Backing up badge, plus another row with the Backup ready badge and Download button

figure.figcaption: Backup Now lifecycle: idle → Backing up → Backup ready → Download (or Discard, or auto-expire after 24 h).

section.p: Daily snapshots capture every database on your instance, plus your uploaded files and attachments, plus your custom modules, plus your instance settings — all in a single restorable unit. We use deduplicated, encrypted cloud storage, which means after the first snapshot every subsequent one only stores what actually changed.
Schedule: the snapshot cron fires every night at 03:00 UTC.
Retention: the most recent 7 snapshots are kept. When the nightly cron creates an 8th, the oldest is dropped automatically.
Pricing: billed monthly, separate from your main subscription, even if your instance is on a yearly plan. The first invoice is prorated to the remaining days in the current month.
Downloadability: snapshots are restore-only — they can't be downloaded directly. For a portable copy of one database, use Backup Now.

ol.li: On your instance detail page, look for the blue "Protect this instance with daily snapshots" prompt at the top.
Click Enable Daily Backups. You land on the activation checkout page.
Review the prorated charge: it's the monthly snapshot fee multiplied by (days remaining in this month ÷ days in this month).
Enter your card details and click Pay now.
Once the payment confirms, you're redirected back to the instance page with a green confirmation banner. Your first snapshot will be taken at the next 03:00 UTC.

img.alt: Enable Daily Backups blue prompt on the instance detail page

div.span: Snapshots are irreversible from the portal. Once enabled, you can't turn them off yourself — contact support if you genuinely need to. We made it this way on purpose so nobody accidentally loses their nightly protection.

ol.li: From the instance detail page, click View Snapshots.
You'll see a table with up to 7 rows, ordered newest first. Each row shows the date and time the snapshot was taken and its size.
On the right of each row is a Restore button — see the next section.

img.alt: Snapshots page with 7 dated rows and restore buttons

figure.figcaption: Snapshots page. The list is capped at 7; the oldest rolls off when an 8th arrives.

section.p: Restoring rolls your instance back in place to exactly the state captured in that snapshot — same databases, same data, same files, same settings. It does not create a new snapshot afterwards, so your list stays exactly as you see it.

Important behaviour: anything that exists on your instance right now but wasn't in the snapshot is removed during the restore. For example: if you took a snapshot last Monday when you had two databases, then created a third database on Tuesday, and now you restore from Monday's snapshot — the third database is deleted as part of the restore. The snapshot's view of the world fully replaces the current state.

ol.li: On the Snapshots page, click Restore on the row you want to go back to.
Confirm by retyping your subdomain into the confirmation field.

Click Yes, restore. A full-screen overlay appears ("Restoring your instance from the snapshot…"). Your instance is offline for the duration — typically a few minutes for a small instance, longer for larger ones.
The page reloads automatically when your instance is back online. Open it from Open Instance on the dashboard and verify your data is back to the expected point in time.

div.span: Your subscription plan is preserved, not restored. If you've upgraded your plan since the snapshot was taken, your restored instance still has the current resources — we don't roll your billing back. Likewise, your custom Python packages and Git repositories stay at their current configuration.

section.p: The instance detail page shows your current plan in the info card at the top:

ul.li: Plan — the plan name (e.g. "Custom", or a named preset).
Billing period — Monthly or Yearly.
Workers / Storage — the resources you've allocated.
Next renewal — the date your next invoice will be issued.

ol.li: Click Change Plan on the instance page.
Adjust the workers / storage sliders to the new size. The order-summary panel on the right shows the price difference, prorated for the remaining days of your current billing cycle.
Click Continue to payment. Pay the prorated upgrade invoice.
Your instance is reconfigured automatically — workers restart with the new resource limits and storage is expanded. No downtime for storage upgrades; a brief restart for worker / RAM upgrades.

section.p: Downgrades are scheduled to take effect at the start of your next billing cycle — until then your instance keeps the current (larger) plan with no service interruption.

ol.li: Click Change Plan on the instance page.
Drag the sliders down to your desired new size.
The order summary shows a scheduled notice — no charge today.
Click Confirm downgrade. From your next renewal date, your renewal invoice uses the smaller plan price.

section.p: Changed your mind before the cycle ends? Click Cancel scheduled downgrade on the instance page to keep the current plan indefinitely.

ul.li: Your main plan renews monthly or yearly depending on what you chose at purchase.
Renewal invoices are issued automatically and emailed to you on the renewal date.
We attempt to charge your saved card automatically if you've enabled card-on-file. Otherwise you'll receive a "Complete payment" email with a link to pay.
Daily snapshots (if enabled) are billed on their own monthly cycle, so yearly-plan customers still see a small monthly snapshot invoice in addition to the yearly renewal.

section.p: If a renewal payment fails, we don't suspend you immediately. Here's the timeline:

ol.li: Day 0 (renewal day): Charge attempt fails. You get an email asking you to update your payment method.
Day 1–5 (grace period): Your instance keeps running. Reminder emails are sent.
Day 6: Instance is suspended — Odoo stops serving traffic, but all data is still intact. A yellow Suspended banner appears on your dashboard with a "Pay now" button.
Day 30: If still unpaid, the instance enters the cancellation flow described in Part 8.

section.p: Pay the outstanding invoice (or update your card) at any point during the first 30 days to bring your instance back online — no data lost.

section.h2: Part 7 — Stopping and starting your instance
section.p: You can pause your instance manually — for example during a long holiday — to free resources without losing data. Stopped instances stop serving traffic but you keep being billed normally.

ol.li: On the instance detail page click Stop. Confirm in the modal.
The status pill switches to Provisioning briefly, then settles on Stopped. Your subdomain returns an "Instance stopped" page to any visitor.
When you're ready, click Start. The instance comes back up in 30–60 seconds, then the status returns to Running.

div.span: Stopping does not pause billing — you're still using a server slot. To actually stop paying, you'd cancel (see Part 8).

section.p: Cancellation is initiated through our support team — drop us a message via the contact page and we'll process it. We kept this manual on purpose so a misclick can't accidentally end your service.
Once we run the cancellation, here's what happens, in order:

ol.li: We take one final snapshot of your current state, so you always have something to restore from if you change your mind.
We tear down your container and release your subdomain.
We delete every database on the instance and the database role.
We prune the snapshot storage to keep only the most recent snapshot — every older snapshot is dropped.
Your instance card on the My Instances page now shows a "Cancelled" badge with a "Reactivate Instance" button.

tr.td: Deleted from the instance, but preserved inside the retained snapshot
Deleted from the instance, but preserved inside the retained snapshot
Released — anyone (including you, on reactivation) can claim it
Kept, ready to restore after reactivation

ol.li: Sign in and open My Instances from the avatar dropdown. Cancelled instances show a "Cancelled" badge.
Click Reactivate Instance on the cancelled instance card.
Pick a new plan and billing period. This is a fresh purchase — you'll be charged from scratch.
Pay the new subscription invoice. Your instance redeploys at the same subdomain (assuming it's still free) with empty databases.

img.alt: Cancelled instance card with the Reactivate button

section.p: Once the reactivated instance is running, here's how you get your data back:

ol.li: From the instance detail page, click View Snapshots. The retained snapshot from your cancellation is listed there.
Click Restore on that row.
Confirm and wait for the restore to complete. Your databases, files and custom modules are all recovered exactly as they were the moment we cancelled.

div.span: If a long time has passed between cancellation and reactivation, the Odoo image version and your custom modules may have moved on. We do our best to keep this compatible, but on rare occasions you may need to run Repair Feature after the restore to re-sync everything.

section.p: Every charge produces an invoice. You can view, pay and download all of them from the portal.

ul.li: Recent invoices column on every instance detail page — scoped to that instance.
Profile menu → Invoices — the complete list across every instance, every period.

ol.li: Open the invoice. Unpaid ones show a yellow Complete payment button.
Click it to land on the secure payment page. Enter your card details and click Pay.
Once paid, the invoice immediately flips to "Paid" status and any gated actions (deploy, reactivation, snapshot activation) proceed automatically.

section.p:
Click the
Download
icon next to any paid invoice. The PDF is suitable for your accounts payable / tax records.

ul.li: Hosting subscription — your main plan (workers + storage), recurring monthly or yearly.
Daily Backups Add-on — the snapshot subscription, recurring monthly.
Storage overage — only if you exceed your plan's storage limit. Per-GB rate, charged on the next renewal.
Upgrade prorated — the difference you pay when upgrading mid-cycle.

section.p: Sometimes you want to peek under the hood to see what your Odoo is doing — for example, when chasing down a slow page or debugging a custom module.

ol.li: On the instance detail page, click View Activity.
A live tail of your instance's activity opens — new lines stream in as they happen.
Use the line count at the top to scroll further back in history.

div.span: The activity log is read-only — you can't change anything from here. It's a diagnostic tool, useful when reporting an issue to support.

section.h3: My Odoo shows an error page on every URL

ol.li: Try Repair Feature on the affected database with base first.
If that doesn't fix it, try all.
If still broken, restore the most recent snapshot from before the issue started (5.6) — provided you have daily snapshots enabled.
Still stuck? Contact support with the activity log tail from View Activity.

section.h3: I can't sign in to my Odoo

ol.li: Make sure the URL is correct: your-subdomain.sudud.com/web.
Verify you're entering the right database name on the database selector page.
If you forgot the admin password, use Reset Password.
If you can sign in as admin but not as a regular user, ask the admin to reset that user's password from inside Odoo (Settings → Users).

section.p: Suspension is caused by either an overdue payment or an expired trial. The instance dashboard tells you which. Pay the outstanding invoice (or upgrade from trial) and the suspension lifts automatically within a minute.
section.h3: My page didn't refresh on its own
section.p: Most actions auto-refresh within 5 seconds of completing. If the in-progress overlay is stuck for more than a few minutes, refresh the browser manually — your action has very likely already finished. If the in-progress banner is still there after that, the operation is genuinely still running; give it another minute.
Backup download links expire 24 hours after the backup was created. If yours has expired, take a fresh backup with Backup Now.

The snapshot list keeps only the last 7. Older snapshots are dropped automatically as newer ones arrive. If you need to keep a specific point-in-time long-term, take a Backup Now of the relevant database(s) and download the zip.
section.h3: I created a database with the wrong name
section.p: Use Delete to remove the wrong one and create a new one with the correct name. We don't support in-place renaming — Odoo database renames have too many side effects to be safe as a one-click operation.
section.h3: I want to move data between instances

ol.li: On the source instance, take a Backup Now of the database you want to move.
Click Download Backup to get the zip.
On the destination instance, go to your-subdomain.sudud.com/web/database/manager.
Use the Restore Database option in the Odoo database manager. Upload the zip and give it a name.

section.h3: How do I report a bug or get help?
section.p: Reach out via the contact page. Include:

ul.li: Your subdomain.
The exact steps to reproduce the issue.
The time (UTC) the issue started.
If relevant: the last 50 lines of your activity log (copy from View Activity).

section.h3: How many databases can I have on one instance?
section.p: There's no hard cap — you're limited by storage. Each empty Odoo database uses around 80 MB; a small business database typically lands between 200 MB and 2 GB. With 20 GB of storage you can comfortably host 10–30 databases.
section.h3: Can I install any Odoo app I want?
section.p: Inside your instance, yes — you have full admin rights, so any module that's bundled with Odoo or available via your own custom repository can be installed. Modules from the Odoo Apps Store can also be installed if you upload them through Odoo's normal Apps → Import flow.
section.h3: Can I use a custom domain instead of the subdomain?
section.p: Yes — contact support to set up a custom domain (e.g. app.your-company.com) pointing at your instance. You keep the your-subdomain.sudud.com URL too; both work simultaneously.
section.h3: Where is my data physically stored?
section.p: Application data lives on our hosting region (cloud provider and country are listed on our public page). Backups are stored in encrypted cloud-object storage in the same region.

Yes — daily snapshots are encrypted with a per-instance key managed by us. On-demand backup zips are stored in the same encrypted bucket; the download link uses a short-lived signed URL so only you can fetch it.
section.h3: Can someone else access my Odoo with my subdomain?
section.p: Only if they have a valid Odoo login on one of your databases. The platform-level portal account (the SUDUD sign-in) and your Odoo logins are completely separate.
section.h3: What's the difference between Backup Now and a snapshot?
section.p: Scope and purpose. Backup Now is a single downloadable database zip. A snapshot is a complete instance-level rollback point. See section 5.1 for a side-by-side table.
section.h3: How do I get a refund?
section.p: Refund policy is described in our Terms of Service. In general: we don't refund used time, but we'll pro-rate cancellation in the first 14 days. Contact support to discuss your situation.
section.h3: Can I have more than one instance?
section.p: Yes — there's no limit. Each instance is a separate subscription on a separate subdomain. Many customers run a production instance + a separate staging instance.

dl.dd: Your private Odoo environment on its own subdomain. You can host multiple databases on one instance.
One self-contained Odoo workspace inside an instance, with its own data, apps and users.
The name before .sudud.com in your URL. You pick it at purchase; it can't be changed later.
A process handling one web request at a time. More workers = more concurrent users.
A free, on-demand, downloadable backup of a single database. Expires in 24 h.
A paid, nightly, in-place restore point covering the entire instance. Last 7 retained.
Rolls your instance back to exactly the state captured in a snapshot. Replaces everything currently on the instance.
Recovery tool that re-installs a feature (Odoo module) when a database's in-app menus aren't reachable.
The 2–5 minute setup phase right after you pay for a new (or reactivated) instance.
Instance status when service is paused due to overdue payment or trial expiry. Data is preserved.
Instance status after we've torn everything down. The most recent snapshot is retained for reactivation.
